Facts.text: St Lucius I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Battle of Vézeronce: the Franks defeat the Burgundians
Battle at Fontenay: Louis & Charles beat their brother Lotharius I
Wibbert of Ravenna chosen as anti-pope Clement III
1st Crusade slaughter Jews of Werelinghofen, Germany
Battle of Ourique: Afonso Henriques (later Afonso I of Portugal) defeats Moors
Sinibaldo dei Fieschi elected as Pope Innocentius IV
Rindfleisch Persecutions, 250 Jews killed in Rothenburg, Germany
Pope Alexander VI accept Treaty of Granada

Diet of Speyer convenes, resulting in the Edict of Speyer, which temporarily suspends the 1521 Edict of Worms (a Papal ban on Luther's teachings); allows Lutheranism and other "reforms" to propagate unimpeded throughout Germany
Book of Concord, standards of Lutheran Church, 1st published
Mentally ill Holy Roman Emperor Rudolf II signs the Treaty of Lieben, giving up Austria, Hungary and Moravia
French government army occupies Fort St Jean d'Angély at La Rochelle
Fork introduced to American dining by Governor Winthrop
A lunar eclipse is the first astronomical event recorded in the American colonies
Spanish garrison at Dunkirk surrenders to French and English
1st recorded monthly Quaker meeting in US held at Sandwich, Massachusetts
Austrian general Prince Eugenius of Savoy named governor of the Austrian Netherlands
General fast because of drought in Massachusetts
French troops occupy Charleroi
Pope Gregory XVI's encyclical "Singulari nos" published
First building constructed at Yerba Buena, now San Francisco
Battle of Oak Grove, Virginia (Orchard, Henrico, French's Field) (Kings's Schoolhouse) Day 1 of 7 Days
US General George Meade replaces General Hooker to be more aggressive
Battle of Petersburg: Union forces begin digging tunnels under Confederate lines
Horse tramway in The Hague, Netherlands opens
First barbed wire patented by Lucien B. Smith of Ohio
Florida, Alabama, Louisiana, Georgia, North Carolina and South Carolina readmitted to US, after the Civil War
US President Andrew Johnson signs a law establishing an eight-hour workday for government workers
American Railway Union under Eugene V Debs goes on strike
Dunhuang manuscripts, including the Diamond Sutra, world's oldest surviving dated book, discovered by Daoist monk Wang Yuanlu in the Mogao Caves, China
Russia mobilizes its army in eastern Siberia preparatory to acting against the Chinese, but also in an attempt to diminish the influence of Japan on the Asian mainland
Boston Beaneater Wiley Piatt is only 20th-century pitcher to lose 2 complete games in one day, falling to Pittsburgh 1-0 & 5-3
NY Yanks & Chicago White Sox end deadlocked game at 6-6 in 18
Tsarist Russian troops in Łódź, Congress Poland crush workers' militia insurrection against Russian occupation; 151-200 workers killed and over 1,000 injured
Railway heir Harry Kendall Thaw shoots and kills architect Stanford White during a performance at the rooftop theatre of Madison Square Garden, later a notorious court case
US Mann Act passed (no women across state lines for immoral purposes)
American Civil War veterans begin arriving at the Great Reunion of 1913
Dutch Parliamentary election (confess party looses majority)
Russian Tsar Nicolaas II fires minister of Foreign affairs Sasonov
Baku-Turkish communist party forms
1st advanced monoplane airliner flight (Junkers F13)
Revolt of Spartacus in Hamburg
League of Nations places Internationall Court of Justice in Hague
Charlie McCartney scores 300 in 205 mins Aust v Notts
Military putsch under General Theodorus Pangulos in Greece
WVO soccer team forms in Oosterhout
NY Giants future Baseball HOF third baseman Freddie Lindstrom ties record of 9 hits in a doubleheader in 12-4 & 8-2 wins over Philadelphia Phillies at the Baker Bowl
Commencement of India's 1st cricket Test v England at Lord's, London (England initially rattled but go on to win)
Hedley Verity takes 15 wickets for England v Australia (7-61 & 8-43)
NY Yank pitcher John Broaca ties record by striking out 5 times
Previously unknown Winchester Manuscript of Sir Thomas Malory's "Le Morte d'Arthur" (1485) announced discovered in the library of Winchester College, England
Cub Augie Galan becomes 1st player to switch hit HRs in a game
US federal minimum wage law guarantees workers 25 cents per hour (rising to 40 cents by 1945) and a maximum 44 hour working week
-26] Soviet counterattack at Rovno, Ukraine
Fair Employment Practices Commission established
Finland declares war on Soviet Union
Finland enters WW2 against the USSR following a massive airstrike the previous day
Germans invade Dubno Poland, giving permission to Ukrainians to do whatever they want to 12,000 Jews living there
British RAF stages a 1,000 bomb raid on Bremen, Germany (WWII)
Arthur Seyss-Inquart, Reichskommissar in the Netherlands, orders mass arrests of Dutch physicians
Crematorium 3 at Birkenau is finished
Racial unrest in Detroit
British assault at Caen, Normandy
Allied landing at Ternate, capital of the Maluku Islands in modern day Indonesia
Imperial General Headquarters in Tokyo announce fall of Okinawa
Norwegian First Government of Einar Gerhardsen forms, appointed by Haakon VII
Heavyweight boxerJoe LouisKOs Tami Mauriello
Joe LouisKOs Jersey Joe Walcott in 11 for heavyweight boxing title, at Yankee Stadium, NYC
"Long-Haired Hare", animated short starring Bugs Bunny, is released in theaters
Presidential election in Syria (some women allowed to vote)
Israeli airline El Al begins transatlantic service  form Israel to New York City's Idlewild Airport
Johnny Pramesa of the Cincinnati Reds and Hank Thompson of the New York Giants each hit inside the park homeruns in 6-4 Reds win at the Polo Grounds, NYC
Dutch General Election: social democratic party wins elections 30% to help form a four-party coalition
86°F in Anchorage, Alaska
First passenger to fly commercially around the world in less than 100 hours
WKNO TV channel 10 in Memphis, Tennessee (PBS) begins broadcasting
Earthquake in northeast Belgium
Madagascar gains independence from France
South Africa beats New Zealand 13-0 in the first rugby test of the series in Johannesburg
Baltimore and California use a record 16 pitchers in a game (8 each) in 14 inns
Iraq Prime Minister Abd al-Karim Qasim demands dominion over Kuwait and the end of the British protectorate; Kuwait disagrees
İnönü government forms in Turkey
Mozambique Liberation Front (FRELIMO) forms
Supreme Court rules NY school prayer unconstitutional
Curnick Ndlovu, South African worker's union leader and African National Congress Leader, is arrested for acts of sabotage
Famous cricket draw at Lord's as England hang on against the Windies
Prince A Taylor becomes first African American Methodist bishop, assigned to the New Jersey Area
WMCA radio in NYC plays The Beatles' "A Hard Days Night" album 10 days prior to its scheduled release date; record company moves release to June 26th
Gyula Kallai succeeds Janos Kádár as premier of Hungary
Kosmos 122, 1st Soviet weather satellite is launched
The Beatles' single "Paperback Writer" goes #1 & stays #1 for 2 weeks
First global satellite television program: "Our World" broadcast features 19 acts representing 19 nations including The Beatles singing "All You Need Is Love"
KPBS TV channel 15 in San Diego, CA (PBS) begins broadcasting
American Pancho González (41) beats Puerto Rican Charlie Pasarell (25) in 5 hour and 12 minute, 112-game marathon at Wimbledon
John Dean begins testifying before Senate Watergate Committee
Udo Beyer of East Germany puts the shot a record 20.47 m
After a prolonged liberation struggle against Portuguese colonial rulers, Mozambique becomes independent as People Republic of Mozambique
Soweto Uprising: in South Africa leaves an estimated 200 to 700 blacks and 2 whites dead following 10 days of rioting
Texas Ranger Toby Harrah is only shortstop not to handle a fielding chance in doubleheader
Roy C. Sullivan of Virginia is struck by lightning for 7th time!
FIFA World Cup Final, El Monumental, Buenos Aires, Argentina: Striker Mario Kempes scores 2 goals as Argentina beats the Netherlands, 3-1 after extra time
First use of the rainbow flag, symbol of gay pride, made by Gilbert Baker at a march in San Francisco
Microsoft is restructured to become an incorporated business in its home state of Washington
Supreme Court upholds male-only draft registration as constitutional
Greece abolishes headshaving of recruits in the military
San Francisco holds its 1st County Fair
ICC Men's Cricket ODI World Cup, Lord's, London: India beats West Indies by 43 runs to win their first trophy; Player of Match: Mohinder Amarnath (IND) 3/12 from 7 overs
Udo Beyer of East Germany sets record for shot put, 22.22 m
Lydia Garrett, 24, crowned 17th Miss Black America
STS 41-D launch attempt scrubbed because of computer problem
Fireworks factory near Hallett, Oklahoma, explodes killing 21
Thurman v City of Torrington decides in favor of Tracey Thurman, 1st woman to sue a police department for violating her civil rights (not protecting against abusive husband)
Former Belgium premier Vanden Boeynants sentenced for fraud
104°F highest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland in June
Expos Pitcher Floyd Youmans suspended for 60 days due to drugs
Kristen Logan, 17, of Mississippi, crowned America's Junior Miss
Roger Rabbit Cartoon Character debuts in "Who Framed Roger Rabbit?"
"Day By Day" last airs on NBC-TV
First US postmark dedicated to Lesbian and Gay Pride (Stonewall, NYC)
Mets' defense does not record a single assist in a 5-1 win over Phils
"Dave Thomas Comedy Show" last airs on CBS-TV
120°F in Phoenix, Arizona
Anti-government riots break out in Lusaka, Zambia
NBC decides to air episodes of "Quantum Leap" for 5 straight days
Supreme Court rules family members cannot end lives of comatose relatives unless those relatives previously made their wishes known
Japan lifts its call for voluntary restraint on expanding trade with South Africa
Six persons are killed and eighteen injured when gunmen open fire on a crowded commuter train in Soweto, South Africa
Alexanders Department store closes all 11 stores in the US
STS 50 launches (Columbia)
NY Islander goalie Billy Smith elected to NHL Hall of Fame
Parliamentary election in Morocco
105°F (40.5°C) at Albuquerque New Mexico
111°F (43.9°C) at El Paso Texas
Cleveland Indians 18 game home win streak ends to Yanks 11-6
FIFA World Cup: 1,500th goal in Cup'd history scored by Caceres of Argentina
Gay Games close in NYC
Japanese premier Tsutomu Hata resigns
Rockies' Andres Galarraga is 4th to HR in 3 consecutive innings
The Khobar Towers bombing in Saudi Arabia kills 19 U.S. servicemen
Galileo, 2nd Callisto Flyby (Orbit 9)
Intelsat 802 Ariane 4 Launch is successful
Jamaica issues warrant for singer Sade, who fails to report to court on charges of failure to obey a cop who signaled her to stop
NHL approves franchises in Nashville, Atlanta, Columbus, and Minnesota-St Paul
Progress M-34 Collides with and damages Mir Space Station
In Clinton v. City of New York, the US Supreme Court decides that the Line Item Veto Act of 1996 is unconstitutional
Peace of Konstanz signed between Hohenstaufen EmperorFrederick IBarbarossa and Italian Lombard League
Siege of Kenilworth Castle begins by forces of KingHenry III; at 172 days, it is one of the longest sieges in English medieval history
The House of Lords and Commons declares KingEdward Vof England as illegitimate based on his parent's alleged bigamous marriage
The Augsburg Confession: German Protestant princes force Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vto hear their Confession of Faith (statement of Lutheran theology)
Thomas Fairfax's New Model Army occupies Oxford
VenetianElena Cornaro Piscopiais awarded a doctorate of philosophy from the University of Padua, the first woman to receive a university doctoral degree or PhD
US PresidentJohn Adamssigns into law the Alien Friends Act, authorizing the president to deport any foreigner deemed "dangerous to the peace and safety of the United States"
Carl Maria von Weber's "Konzertstück in f, for Piano and Orchestra" premieres
Republican Convention in Chicago nominatesBenjamin Harrison
Boer leaderPaul Krugermeets British High Commissioner, Sir Henry Loch, on Pretoria station in South Africa and accompanies him to his hotel to discuss the grievances of the Uitlanders (Foreigners)
Igor Stravinsky's ballet "The Firebird" for the Ballets Russes premieres at the Opéra de Paris, Paris
US PresidentHerbert Hooverauthorizes building of Boulder Dam (HooverDam)
NY YankeeLou Gehrighits for the cycle beating White Sox 11-2
Future world heavyweight boxing championJoe Louismoves to 20-0 with 6th round KO of former championPrimo Carneraof Italy at Yankee Stadium, NYC
"A Tisket, A Tasket" byElla Fitzgeraldwith Chick Webb hits #1
James F. Byrnesis admitted to the US Supreme Court
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltsigns Executive Order 8802, which forbids racial discrimination in the defense industry
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchilltravels from US to London
Major GeneralDwight Eisenhowerappointed commander of US forces in Europe
First version ofAnne Frank's diary "Het Achterhuis" is published in the Netherlands
Harry Trumansigns Displaced Persons Bill (205,000 Europeans to US)
First color TV broadcast,Arthur Godfreyhosts a musical variety special titled "Premiere" from CBS NYC to a network of five East Coast cities
"Imogene CocaShow" last airs on NBC-TV
Notre Dame du Haut chapel designed byLe Corbusieris consecrated in Ronchamp, France
"Jonathan WintersShow" last airs on NBC-TV
JFKmakes an address in the Assembly Hall at the Paulskirche in Frankfurt, West Germany
Dmitri Shostakovich's 13th Symphony premieres in Leningrad, Russia
Bobby Bondshits a grand slam in his 1st major league game (San Francisco Giants)
Stevie Wonderreleases single "Where I'm Coming From"
Russian communist party leaderLeonid Brezhnevvisits France
Barry White's "Can't Get Enough of Your Love Babe" is released
Supernatural horror film "The Omen" starringGregory Peckand Lee Remick premieres in the US
The Defense Council of Uganda declaresIdi Aminto be president of the country for life
Failed attack on NATO commanderAlexander HaigJr. in Obourg, Belgium by German terrorist Rolf Klemens Wagner, a former member of the Red Army Faction
Science-fiction film "Blade Runner", directed byRidley Scott, starringHarrison Fordand Rutger Hauer, is released
South African PresidentP. W. Bothaissues a new Proclamation again placing Ingwavuma under government control
US Secretary of StateAlexander HaigJr. resigns, replaced byGeorge P. Shultz

Warner Bros Records releases the sixth studio album by Prince, "Purple Rain"; his commercial peak, the film soundtrack album sells over 25 million copies, tops the charts in five countries, and wins two Grammy Awards and an Academy Award
MLB Philadelphia Phillies giveSteve Carlton, 41, his unconditional release
PopeJohn Paul IIreceives Austrian PresidentKurt Waldheim
MLB playerCal Ripken Jr. plays in his 1,000th consecutive game
African National Congress leaderNelson Mandelameets with PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushat the White House
Martina Navratilovawins her record 100th singles match at Wimbledon, defeating Elna Reinach of South Africa 4-6, 6-2, 6-4 in the first round
Slovenia and Croatia declare independence from Yugoslavia
"Late Night withDavid Letterman" airs for the last time on NBC-TV
Kim Campbellbecomes the 19th Prime Minister of Canada (although she would remain in office for less than five months)
"Independence Day", directed by Roland Emmerich, and starringWill Smith,Bill PullmanandJeff Goldblumpremieres in Westwood, California
Jay-Zreleases his debut studio album "Beyond Reasonable Doubt" with Roc-A-Fella Records and Priority Records
Christies auctions off some of the wardrobe ofDiana, Princess of Wales, for $5.5 million
Danielle Steel’s romantic novel “Special Delivery” is published
NBA Draft: Wake Forest power forwardTim Duncanfirst pick by San Antonio Spurs
Virginia becomes 10th state to ratify US constitution

Battle of the Little Bighorn: US 7th Cavalry under Brevet Major General George Armstrong Custer is wiped out by Sioux and Cheyenne warriors led by ChiefsCrazy Horseand Sitting Bull in what becomes famously known as "Custer's Last Stand"
North Korea invades South Korea, beginning the Korean War
Beatrice of England, Duchess of Brittany, daughter of Henry III of England, born in Bordeaux, France (d. 1275)
William Montacute, 2nd Earl of Salisbury, English military leader, born in Donyatt, Somerset, England (d. 1397)
Johanna II, Queen of Naples (1414-35), born in Zadar, Dalmatia (d. 1435)
Wilhelm Fabry, German surgeon (Father of German surgery), born in Hilden, Germany (d. 1634)
John Albert Vasa, Polish bishop, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1634)
Francesco Araja, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1770)
Joseph-François Foulon, French politician, born in Saumur, Maine-et-Loire, France (d. 1789)
Thomas Pennant, Welsh naturalist, born in Downing, Wales (d. 1798)
Shah Alam II, 17th Mughal Emperor (1760-1806), born in Shahjahanabad, Mughal Empire (d. 1806)
Benvenuto Robbio San Raffaele, Italian composer, born in Chieri, Turin, Kingdom of Sardinia (d. 1797)
Princess Natalia Alexeievna of Russia, born in Prenzlau, Brandenburg, Prussia (d. 1776)
Ferdinando Giorgetti, Italian composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1867)
David Douglas, Scottish botanist (Douglas fir), born in Scone, Perthshire, Scotland (d. 1834)[1]
William High Keim, American politician and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Reading, Pennsylvania (d. 1862)
Gabriel Auguste Daubrée, French geologist, born in Metz, France (d. 1896)
James Dunwoody Bulloch, Confederacy's chief foreign agent in Great Britain during the American Civil War, born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 1901)
Georges Courteline [Moineau], French playwright, born in Tours, France (d. 1929)
Gustave Charpentier, French opera composer (Louise), born in Dieuze, France (d. 1956)
Vasily Georgiyevich Wrangell, Russian composer, born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1901)
Émile Francqui, Belgian soldier and diplomat, born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1935)
Walther Hermann Nernst, Prussian physicist and chemist (Nobel 1920), born in Briesen, West Prussia (d. 1941)
Jean Gallon, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1959)
Daniel-Henry Kahnweiler, German-French art dealer and publisher, born in Mannheim, German Empire (d. 1979)
Henry "Hap" Arnold, American commanding general, US Army Air Force (WWII), born in Gladwyne, Pennsylvania (d. 1950)
George Abbott, American theater producer and film director (Damn Yankees; The Pajama Game), born in Forestville, New York (d. 1995)
Ethel Glenn Hier, American composer, born in Madisonville, Ohio (d. 1971)
Margriet Baers, Flemish philosopher and social worker, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1922)
Charlotte Greenwood, American actress (Oklahoma; Moon over Miami), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1978)
Hans Marchwitza, German writer and communist, born in Scharley, Upper Silesia (d. 1965)
Hermann Oberth, German physicist (founded modern astronautics), born in Hermannstadt, Austria-Hungary (d. 1989)
Basil Radford, British actor (The Lady Vanishes, Whiskey Galore, Night Train), born in Chester England (d. 1952)
Hans Barth, German-born American composer, born in Leipzig, German Empire (d. 1956)
Kay [Katherine Linn] Sage, American painter and poet, born in Albany, New York (d. 1963)
Arthur Tracy [Avrom Tracovutsky], Ukrainian-American radio personality, vocalist, and accordion player (The Street Singer), born in Kamenetz-Podolsky, Russian Empire (now Ukraine) (d. 1997)
I. D. du Plessis, South African writer and director (3rd World), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1981)
Adolf Brunner, Swiss composer (Markus-Passion), born in Zurich, Switzerland (d. 1992)
Prince Yasuhito Chichibu, brother of Emperor Showa, born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 1953)
Anne Revere, American actress (A Place in the Sun), born in New York City (d. 1990)
Ian Cromb, New Zealand cricket all-rounder (5 Tests, 1 x 50, 8 wickets; Canterbury), born in Christchurch, New Zealand (d. 1984)
Mary Livingstone [Sadya Marcowitz/Marks], American radio actress, comedienne, and wife of Jack Benny (The Jack Benny Program), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1983)
Roger Livesey, Welsh actor (Drums, The Life and Death of Colonel Blimp), born in Barry, Wales (d. 1976)
J. Hans D. Jensen, German physicist (atomic nucleus-Nobel 1963), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1973)
Willard Van Orman Quine, American philosopher and logician in the analytic tradition, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2000)
Daniel Fuchs, American screenwriter (Summer in Williamsburg), born in Lower East Side, Manhattan (d. 1993)
Reed Hadley, American actor (Zorro's Fighting Legion, Racket Squad, Shock, Grand Canyon), born in Petrolia, Texas (d. 1974)
William H. Stein, American biochemist (Nobel 1972), born in New York (d. 1980)
Virginia Lacy Jones, American librarian and presidential advisor, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1984)
William T. Cahill, American politician, 46th Governor of New Jersey (1970-74), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Cyril Fletcher, British comedian, born in Watford, Hertfordshire, England (d. 2005)
Peter Lind Hayes, American comedian and singer (Peter Lind Hayes Show), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1998)
Philip Toynbee, British journalist and writer (Savage Days, Barricade), born in Oxford, England (d. 1981)
Ken Mayers, American actor (Little Big Man, Dick Tracey, Space Patrol), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1985)
Celia Franca [Franks], British-Canadian ballet dancer, choreographer, co-founder of National Ballet of Canada and its artistic director (1951-75), born in London, England (d. 2007)
Peter Wishart, English composer, born in Crowborough, England (d. 1984)
Johnny Smith, American cool jazz guitarist ("Moonlight In Vermont"; "Walk, Don't Run"), born in Birmingham, Alabama (d. 2013)
Dorothy Gilman, American mystery writer, born in New Brunswick, New Jersey (d. 2012)
Nicholas Mosley, British novelist, biographer and son of Oswald Mosley, born in London (d. 2017)
Sam Francis, American painter (Basel Mural), born in San Mateo, California (d. 1994)
Tony Parker, British oral historian, born in Stockport, Cheshire (d. 1996)
Madan Mohan [Kohli], Indian singer, music director, and Hindi film score composer, born in Baghdad, Mandatory Iraq (d. 1975)
Clifton Chenier, American blues singer (Bayou Blues), born in Opelousas, Louisiana (d. 1978)
June Lockhart, American actress (Lassie, Lost in Space, Petticoat Junction), born in New York City
Robert Venturi, American architect (Vanna Venturi House) and theorist (inspired Postmodernism), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2018)[1]
Ted Stepien, American businessman and basketball team owner, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2007)
Ziggy Talent, American saxophonist and novelty singer (Vaughn Monroe Orchestra - "Sam, You Made the Pants Too Long"), born in Manchester, New Hampshire (d. 1997)
Ingeborg Bachmann, Austrian author and poet (Gott von Manhattan), born in Klagenfurt, Austria (d. 1973)
Alexei Abrikosov, Russian-born American physicist (Nobel Prize 2003), born in Moscow (d. 2017)
Jacob Druckman, American Pulitzer Prize-winning composer (Animus; Windows; Vox Humana), and educator, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1996)
Michel Brault, Canadian filmmaker (Direct Cinema - pioneered hand-held camera work), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2013)[1]
Peyo[Pierre Culliford], Belgiancartoonist(The Smurfs; Johan et Pirlouit), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1992)
William Joseph Russo, American trombonist, jazz, classical and theater composer (Titans Symphony) and educator, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2003)
George Murdock, American actor, born in Salina, Kansas (d. 2012)
Hugo Gabriel Gryn, British Reform rabbi, born in Berehovo, Czechoslovakia (d. 1996)
V.P. [Vishwanath Pratap] Singh, Indian politician, 7th Prime Minister of India (1989-90), born in Allahabad, British India (d. 2008)
Peter Blake, British visual artist who designed The Beatles Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band album cover, born in Dartford, Kent, England
Álvaro Siza Vieira, Portuguese architect, born in Matosinhos, Portugal
Michelangelo Pistoletto, Italian artist (glass painter), born in Biella, Italy
Willy Rodriguez, West Indian cricketer (occasional leg-spin bat for WI in 60's), born in St Clair, Port of Spain, Trinidad and Tobago
Kurt Schwertsik, Austrian classical hornist, contemporary composer (Ein Kleines Requiem; Draculas Haus und Hofmusik), and educator, born in Vienna, Austria
Larry Kramer, American playwright and AIDS activist, born in Bridgeport, Connecticut (d. 2020)
Wynand Breytenbach, South African soldier and politician, Deputy Minister of Defense (1986-94), born in Orange Free State, South Africa (d. 2002)
B. J. Habibie, 3rd President of Indonesia (1998-99) who liberalized Indonesia's press and political party laws, born in Parepare, South Sulawesi, Dutch East Indies (d. 2019)
Paul Nowee, Dutch writer of children's and youth books. (Eagle's Eye), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Baron Wolman, American photographer, founding chief photographer for Rolling Stone, born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 2020)

Marabel Morgan, American author (The Total Woman), born in Crestline, Ohio
Obuchi Keizo, Prime Minister of Japan (1998-2000), born in Nakanojō, Empire of Japan
Allen Fox, American tennis player, born in Los Angeles, California
Harold Melvin, American soul and R&B singer (The Blue Notes - "If You Don't Know Me By Now"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1997)
William Smith, South African teacher who hosted TV program "The Learning Channel", born in Grahamstown, South Africa[1]
A. J. Quinnell [Philip Nicholson], English writer (Man on Fire), born in Nuneaton, Warwickshire, England (d. 2005)
Clint Warwick [Albert Eccles], English bassist (Moody Blues, 1964-66 - "Go Now"), born in Birmingham, England (d. 2004)

Eddie Large [McGinnis], British comedian (Little and Large), born in Glasgow, Scotland (d. 2020)
Joe Chambers, American jazz drummer, vibraphonist, and composer, born in Chester, Pennsylvania
Michel Tremblay, Canadian novelist and playwright (Les Belles-Sœurs), born in Montreal, Quebec
Patrick Michael Mitchell, one of FBI's most wanted, born in Ottawa, Ontario
Bill Moggridge [William], British designer (1st laptop computer), born in London, England (d. 2012)
Carly Simon, American Grammy and Academy Award-winning singer-songwriter ("Anticipation"; "You're So Vain"), born in New York City
Chris Huston, British-born recording engineer and guitarist, born in North Wales, England
Gerrit "Ger" Zijlstra, Dutch sculptor, born in Utrecht, Netherlands
Robert Charlebois, Canadian folk-rock singer, composer ("Les Ailes d'un Ange"), and writer, born in Montreal, Quebec
Harry Womack, American gospel and R&B singer and bassist (Womack Brothers; The Valentinos - "It's All Over Now"), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1974)
Labi Siffre, British singer-songwriter, and poet ("It Must Be Love"; "Crying Laughing Loving Lying"), born in Hammersmith, London, England
Allen Lanier, American rock keyboardist, guitarist and vocalist (Blue Oyster Cult), born on Long Island, New York (d. 2013)
Ian McDonald, British rock musician (saxophone, keyboards, guitar) (King Crimson, 1968-69; Foreigner, 1976-80), born in Osterly, Middlesex, England (d. 2022)[1]
Roméo Dallaire, Canadian senator and humanitarian, born in Denekamp, Netherlands
Ulrik le Fevre, Danish soccer left winger (37 caps; Vejle Boldklub, Borussia Mönchengladbach, Club Brugge), born in Vejle, Denmark (d. 2024)
Jimmie Walker, American comedian (JJ-Good Times, At Ease), born in The Bronx, New York
Broery Marantika [Simon Pesulima], Indonesian blues and jazz singer, and actor, born in Ambon, Maluku, Japanese-occupied East Indies (d. 2000)
Michael Lembeck, American actor (Max-One Day at a Time), born in Brooklyn, New York
Brenda Sykes, American actress (Ozzie's Girls; Getting Straight), born in Shreveport, Louisiana
Kene Holliday, American actor (Tyler-Matlock, Carter Country), born in New York City
Michael Barbiero, American disco and heavy metal sound engineer, mixer, record producer, and studio owner, born in New York City
Patrick Tambay, French auto racer (Can Am Challenge Cup Series 1977, 80; 123 x F1 GP 1977-86), born in Paris, France (d. 2022)
Phyllis George, American beauty queen (Miss America, 1971), sportscaster (CBS, 1974-85), and First Lady of Kentucky, 1979-83, born in Denton, Texas (d. 2020)
Leonid Georiyevich Ivanov, Russian cosmonaut (Soyuz 33), born in Safonovo, Russia (d. 1980)
Nitza Saul, Israeli actress, born in Tel Aviv, Israel
Tatyana Averina, Russian speed skater 1K, 3K (Olympic gold 1976), born in Nizhny Novgorod, Russia (d. 2001)
Lee Wilkof, American stage and screen character actor (WEB; Delta House; Hart to Hart; Max Headroom), born in Canton, Ohio
Brian MacLeod, Canadian rock guitarist, keyboardist and vocalist (Chilliwack), born in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada (d. 1992)
Martin Gerschwitz, German session and touring rock keyboardist (Lita Ford, Walter Trout, Eric Burdon), born in Solingen, West Germany
Radka Toneff, Norwegian jazz singer (It Don't Come Easy), born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1982)

Tim Finn, New Zealand rock guitarist, singer, songwriter, and keyboardist (Split Enz - "I Got You"; Crowded House - "Don't Dream It's Over"), and theatre and film composer ("Puberty Blues"), born in Te Awamutu, New Zealand
Ian Davis, Australian cricket batsman (15 Tests, 1 x 100; NSW CA), born in Sydney, Australia
